author: @gen81465
text: I know this video is quite old, and you probably haven't worked on this in a bit, but I have some questions, thoughts and ideas. When you ran the seawater over the graphene sheet, you mentioned that it continued to produce electricity. If there were a way to recirculate the same seawater over it, would it continue to generate electricity, or is there something that is somehow "extracted" from the seawater each time, that would eventually no longer be there? My thought is that you can only use the same seawater for a limited time, before something is "missing"; or am I the one missing something? I would like to see an experiment something like this: have the coated sheet standing vertically (or angled). A tray at the bottom catches the seawater after it has been run over the sheet, and a micropump recirculates the water to run over it again. I wonder how long it would keep producing electricity, using the same seawater. I've tried to ask this question of several people who work in the engineering field, but all I get is ridiculed for not understanding all the concepts completely. I'm hoping you will take a moment to respond. You seem like a different sort of person, who doesn't mind answering questions, every now and then, about concepts that are probably quite simple for you, but may not be as simple for others. You don't seem condescending towards those just trying to improve their knowledge, and that attitude is greatly appreciated. Thanks. George (gen81465)

author: @joeglory425
text:
If one assumes the electrical energy comes from a conversion of the kinetic energy of the wave action, then there's really not much available to convert (in the specific example you showed) and I wouldn't expect much output.  Where it could get interesting is with high pressure and velocity water flows.  Again if you assume there's a transfer of kinetic energy to electrical the next question is how far reaching is the effect?  Does it only affect just the first few boundary layers of water, or out to 1 millimeter, 1 centimeter, or all the way out to 10 meters?  Imagine capturing 50 percent of the kinetic energy of salt water flowing through a 10 meter diameter pipe with just a coating on the pipe wall.  It doesn't seem realistic until you consider that a wind turbine blade does just that.  It's capturing not just the energy that goes past the blade but the energy of the entire volume of wind that passes through the area of rotation.

Does the effect still work if the coated substrate is a conductor like aluminum, copper or iron?  For large amounts of power this may be a requirement.

Hi Robbert, if you edge the graphene platelets with say iodine in a ball mill before making your ink, I think you will find that things will take off in multiples.
This type of tech: will make a cell far more efficient and so easy to do at low cost. Your base material could be a cheap porus ceramic, made from sea sand for example, 1-1.5mm thick.
I think you may have already edged your graphene platelets with what ever you made the graphene with to start with. There will be an ion exchange and that is where your current is coming from.
I believe that this is the basis of the BLOOM BOX cell, my take on the BOX, as you know I am heavily into this type of tech:
E-mail me if you want to and I can give you more
centraflow@gmail.com

regards
Mike

author: @A1Skeptic
text:
Toss two different metals into salt water and you'll get a small current as they corrode. (given)
Therefore, it is necessary to exclude current produced by this "salt water battery" effect, to properly evaluate the power produced by the graphene sheet itself. So (for instance) make up another PET sheet with copper strips but no graphene and test that with the same test meter/clips. Only then can you celebrate the motion of the ocean.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure - but did you miss the point - at the moment no one is trying to properly evaluate it - we are just noting an interesting effect - if you saw the first vid you will have seen isolated clips and no metal strips - so though I agree with your comments - i don't agree with your tone - putting a downer on something just because you like to put a downer on something is not a good way to move forward mate. Like I say i think it is you who missed the point here.

replies.author: @A1Skeptic
replies.text:
I wasn't "putting a downer on something just because you like to put a downer on something" I was pointing out a significant problem with your initial field test that could account for the majority of the current detected. I did watch the first vid. As I recall, the voltage was measured without a load resistor and so cannot be compared directly to the ocean test. In the ocean test, the graphene test sheet without the copper strips put out ~ 7mv, compared to ~27mv for the test sheet with copper strips. That data led to my note of caution as to the source of the additional current from the sheet with copper strips in the field test. Bare metal in salt water can easily produce HIGHER voltages/currents than those you recorded. You can blame the messenger if you like, but Occam's razor says it's a poorly designed battery until proven otherwise. And since your test sheet was neither designed,  or intended to produce current by this effect, it is an absolutely valid point that this effect must be positively excluded for the true results to be known. 

The potential of your research is indeed exciting. I would be thrilled if further testing actually proved that all of the current you were seeing was coming from the interaction of the graphene and water, and not from electro-chemical reactions that have been familiar to science for the last two centuries. I didn't peg you as someone who would want to mislead anyone as you have so far come across as an excellent teacher and mentor. I apologize for being a bit snarky, but I WAS paying attention in class, no?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
A1Skeptic
refer later vids that take up your points

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
A1Skeptic
I knew you wouldn't be able to resist a long come back - read the referred papers - resist being snarky or face deletion

replies.author: @A1Skeptic
replies.text: Wouldn't it be a lot more satisfying to just make up a sheet with copper strips, repeat the ocean test, and get solid data quantifying the source of current?

author: @FredGandt
text:
*Robert went to the beach for a paddle...*
In your recent Generating Electricity From Salt Water And Graphene you say when you check the voltmeter after pouring water over the test sheet, that it's still generating ".8" volts.
I'm pretty sure if that reading's correct, that the little test sheet managed "2.3" volts during that test. Is that correct?
Assuming so, it looks as if speed is more important than area, but coverage is clearly vital too, and combined they could return quite useful current!
This sea side test definitely proves that the _"just stick it in the sea mate"_ approach works, and *I totally agree that this electricity production method deserves attention and development*.
The venturi tube idea would seem well worth a go, and perhaps you might find a local boat fisherman willing to take a coated tube for a ride (drag along side or behind)?
The Swale is just up the road from you too; I believe there's quite a flow through there.
:-)
Natural salty mineral springs might be tapped too.
It's very exciting that something so simple and cheap can be employed so easily to such great potential effect.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think the remaining voltage was from a double layer capacitance that is residual to the sheet once it is removed from the water and the resistor across the terminals leads to the discharge of that voltage which is why in this sea trial it disappears but in the lab trial it didn't. Mt first thought is to optimism the ink and then look at practical deployment methods. So, I will start to look at the ink more closely on monday

replies.author: @FredGandt
replies.text:
It wasn't the residual voltage that interested me so much as "the reading" since if ".8" was correct, then during the actual test, the reading was (at max) about "2.3" volts -  a fair few more millis than you got at the beach.

Damn good stuff. Have a good weekend :-)
